docs: So, here’s the deal: on February 24, 2022, Russia shocked everyone by invading Ukraine, kicking off a brutal conflict that’s still going on. Vladimir Putin, Russia's President, has given a bunch of different reasons for why he decided to invade. Let’s break down what he’s said, how it lines up with reality, and some other likely reasons why Russia decided to send its troops into a country that’s independent and sovereign. You see, Ukraine used to be part of the Soviet Union, but it declared its independence and solidified that choice with a referendum just days before the USSR fell apart in December 1991. Since then, Ukraine has proudly kept its independence. But Putin still insists that Ukraine is part of Russia and doesn’t see it as a separate nation. He even told then-US President George W. Bush back in 2008 that Ukraine wasn’t really a country at all. Stephen Hall, a Russian politics expert over at the University of Bath in the UK, mentioned that a lot of Russians still think this way, and it’s not just the Kremlin pushing that idea. Hall pointed out that Russia views Kyiv, the capital of Ukraine, as the “mother of Russian cities,” and for Putin, having that city outside of Russia just doesn't sit right. He also noted that for Russia to maintain its status as a great power with a long history, it needs to claim Ukraine. Without it, Hall argues, Russia can’t really talk about a thousand years of history since Kyiv was around 1,200 years ago when Moscow was still just a forest.

So, back in July 2021, Putin wrote this essay called "On the Historical Unity of Russians and Ukrainians," where he basically said that Russians and Ukrainians are really "one people." Pretty bold claim, right? Leading up to the invasion, he went on to argue that Ukraine was something the Russian Bolsheviks came up with and that it never really had its own true statehood. An American historian named Timothy Snyder called his views imperialism, while British journalist Edward Lucas labeled it historical revisionism. There are also many people pointing out that the Russian leadership has a pretty skewed understanding of both modern Ukraine and its history, and these misconceptions have been pushed and reinforced by state policies. 

Now, in March and April 2021, Russia started ramping up military activity near the border with Ukraine. Then, from October 2021 to February 2022, they did it again, this time in both Russia and Belarus. Interestingly, members of the Russian government kept insisting that they had no plans to invade or attack Ukraine. This included government spokesman Dmitry Peskov back in November 2021, Deputy Foreign Minister Sergei Ryabkov in January 2022, and even the Russian ambassador to the US, Anatoly Antonov, in February 2022. Oh, and let’s not forget the Russian ambassador to the Czech Republic, Alexander Zmeevsky, who said something similar just a few days later.

You know, back in early 2021, Zelenskyy really took a stand against pro-Russian oligarchs in Ukraine, and one of the biggest names on that list was Viktor Medvedchuk, who’s pretty tight with Putin. Now, let’s rewind a bit to 2014 when the Ukrainian regions of Donetsk and Luhansk decided to break away from Ukraine, and the Ukrainian government labeled their leaders as Russian-backed terrorists. After things went south with NATO and the West in late February, Putin recognized these regions as independent and sent in troops to “keep the peace.” Just days after that, Russia kicked off a full-scale invasion of Ukraine, starting in the eastern Donbas region. By June 2022, around 8 million Ukrainians had been forced to flee their homes, and by May 2023, over 8.2 million had left the country altogether. This has turned into the largest refugee crisis in Europe since World War II. 

On top of all that, there’s been significant environmental damage, which some are calling ecocide, and that’s led to food shortages around the globe. Before the invasion, Russian troops were gathering close to Ukraine's borders, while Russian officials were insisting there were no plans to attack. Then, Vladimir Putin came out and said it was a "special military operation" to help the breakaway regions of Donetsk and Luhansk, where local forces were already clashing with Ukraine. He claimed the mission was to "demilitarise" and "denazify" Ukraine, but he also pushed some pretty extreme views about Ukraine's right to exist and made false claims about the government being run by neo-Nazis who were persecuting ethnic Russians.

So, here's the thing: Putin made some pretty bold claims, saying they were dealing with "humiliation and genocide" from the Kyiv regime, which wasn't true at all. He also insisted that Ukraine's government was made up of neo-Nazis controlled by the West, that they were working on nuclear weapons, and that NATO was setting up military stuff in Ukraine to threaten Russia. He argued that Russia wanted to "demilitarize and denazify" Ukraine. Interestingly, he claimed he had no intention of occupying Ukraine and even backed the idea that the Ukrainian people should have the right to decide for themselves. But right after he made his announcement, Russian missiles started hitting targets across Ukraine, and troops came in from the north, east, and south.
